<!-- Contract for skills (stable regardless of content changes below):
     * generate-claude-md links to this file from every generated CLAUDE.md
       ("Follow reference/design-principles.md") — it does NOT inline it.
     * implement-feature loads this file when making design decisions.
     * Content here is human-owned and never regenerated by any skill. -->

# Design Principles (shared reference)

Single source of truth for preferred design patterns across all skills in this
framework. The **architecture-advisor** references this file but does not apply it
beyond noting the ports-and-adapters expectation in its ADRs. The **C4 diagram /
design skill** applies it in detail when decomposing containers into components. The
**cicd-bootstrap** skill mirrors the ports-and-adapters idea at the pipeline level
(build once, thin deploy adapters).

Goals, in priority order: **decoupling, modularity, maintainability, observability.**
Patterns are means to these ends — apply one only when it serves a goal for the system
at hand, and prefer the simplest structure that does. In Go, favour interfaces defined
at the consumer and plain constructor injection; in Python, favour protocols/ABCs and
explicit constructor arguments. Avoid DI containers and pattern frameworks — the
minimal-dependency invariant applies to design patterns too.

## Core patterns

### Ports & adapters (hexagonal) — the structural default
Core domain logic depends only on interfaces ("ports") it defines; everything external
(HTTP, DB, queues, third-party APIs, FaaS runtimes) is an adapter implementing or
calling those ports. This is **mandatory in spirit** for every system here because it is
what makes the deployment-flexibility invariant real: swapping VM/container/serverless/
Kubernetes only ever touches adapters. C4 component diagrams should make the
core/adapter boundary visible.

### Dependency injection (constructor injection only)
Components receive their dependencies as interface/protocol values at construction —
no globals, no service locators, no DI framework. Wire the object graph in one
place (`main.go` / `main.py` composition root). This is what makes integration tests
cheap (inject fakes) and keeps modules independently comprehensible for an
AI-assisted, mixed-experience team.

### Repository pattern
Data access behind a port per aggregate/entity (`UserRepository`), with the concrete
SQL/ORM adapter behind it. Apply when there is meaningful domain logic to isolate from
persistence; skip the ceremony for trivial CRUD where it would just echo the table.
Repositories are the seam for integration tests and for the SQLite-locally /
PostgreSQL-in-production option.

### Strategy pattern
Interchangeable behaviours behind a common interface, selected at composition time or
runtime (e.g., pricing rules, export formats, notification channels, per-tenant
policies). Reach for it when the interview/ADRs revealed variation points; do not
pre-abstract behaviours that have exactly one implementation and no revisit trigger.

## Observability (built in, not bolted on)
- Structured logging from the start: `log/slog` (Go), `logging` with a JSON formatter
  (Python) — stdlib first.
- Every request/job carries a correlation ID through ports and adapters.
- Health (`/healthz`) and readiness endpoints in every service — these are what make
  the artifact portable across orchestrators.
- Metrics/tracing via OpenTelemetry **only when** an ADR records the need; otherwise
  logs + health endpoints are the baseline.
- Observability lives in adapters and middleware, never inside domain logic.

### Security
- server-side authentication and authorization should happen through centralized mechanisms like middleware
- prefer JSON Web Tokens (JWT) for secure, stateless user authentication. Ensure proper signature validation, appropriate expiration windows, and secure token storage.
- Prioritize explicit permission-based checks over generic role-based access control (RBAC). Verify if a user has the specific permission required for an action (e.g., `can_edit_repo`), rather than just checking a broad role (e.g., `Developer`).
- deny access by default unless explicit permissions are met.
- security logic will fail-closed, failures throw explicit errors and log them securely without leaking sensitive system data.

## Applying these in C4 diagrams
- Container diagrams: show adapters (HTTP API, workers, CLI) as entry points around a
  core.
- Component diagrams: name ports (interfaces) explicitly on the boundaries; group
  components by module boundary from the architecture ADR, not by file layout.
- Annotate variation points (strategies) and persistence seams (repositories) so the
  diagram explains *where the system is designed to change*.

## Adding to this file
New patterns are welcome, but each entry must state: the goal it serves (decoupling /
modularity / maintainability / observability), when to apply it, and when it is
over-engineering. Keep this the only place pattern guidance lives — skills link here
rather than copying.
